rulings.Case Title: DEAN TAYLOR VS. JERE PATTERSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Jere Patterson And Marcia White'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Appear For Second Deposition And For Leave To Complete Fact Discovery At A Date Later Than Currently Set (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 1, Defendants Jere Patterson And Marcia White'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Appear For Second Deposition And For Leave To Complete Fact Discovery At A Date Later Than Currently Set (Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling) The motion is DENIED to the extent it seeks to compel production of unredacted email addresses. Parties are entitled to discover the identity of potential witnesses, CCP 2017.010, and email addresses do not raise such privacy interests that require redaction, Puerto v. Sup. Ct. (2008) 158 Cal.App.4th 1212. Nevertheless, the notice of motion is only based on CCP 2025.480 (depositions), and the documents with redacted email addresses were produced in response to later served RFPs. Plaintiff's request to strike Ex F to the Luk Declaration is GRANTED. That attachment is not what is described in the declaration and appears settlement-related. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DEAN TAYLOR VS. JERE PATTERSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Appear For Second Deposition And For Leave To Complete Fact Discovery At A Date Later Than Currently Set
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 1, (part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling) DEFENDANTs JERE PATTERSON and MARCIA WHITE's Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Appear For Second Deposition And For Leave To Complete Fact Discovery At A Date Later Than Currently Set Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is GRANTED in part. Deposition to occur by June 5, 2019. CCP 2025.480, 2024.050. Neither the Constitutional right of privacy nor the "taxpayer privilege" are absolute. Puerto v. Sup. Ct. (2008) 158 Cal.App.4th 1242; Fortunato v. Sup. Ct. (2003) 114 Cal. App. 4th 475. How a plaintiff calculates loss of income or earning capacity is discoverable. So, questions that go to underlying facts and records are permissible but those related to tax calculation are not. Question 1: The "no" appears responsive. No further testimony required. Question 2: How Plaintiff tracks income is not privileged. The "no" is non-responsive. Further testimony permitted. Question 3: To the extent "how you figure out how to pay your taxes?" relates to information used to calculate income, it is permissible. To the extent it seeks return data (adjusted gross income, deductions, etc.), the information would be privileged. Question 4: Whether Plaintiff hired an accountant or tax preparer is not privileged. Such persons may have discoverable non-privileged records. Questions 5, 6, 7: To the extent the question seeks information Plaintiff used to prepare his tax returns, it is permissible. To the extent it calls for information about how Plaintiff considered that information in preparing a return, it is privileged. Questions 8, 9, 10: Whether Plaintiff reported income/filed returns is not relevant to damages. Objection sustained. Question 11: The deponent answered the question and related follow-up. No further testimony required. See Part 2 of 2 for entire tentative ruling. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LAUREN D. LUCERO VS. LIANA TAHIR ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Lauren D. Luceros Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set One, Nos. 36-98, 100-103, 110-113, 119-127 And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANTs LIANA TAHIR and HENDRY TAHIR's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Lauren D. Luceros Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set One, Nos. 36-98, 100-103, 110-113, 119-127 And For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is DENIED. Attorney Alioto's letter of April 18, 2019, states that "Plaintiff has provided all documentary evidence in her possession, all facts currently known to her that support her contentions and damages (in response to Special Interrogatories as well as Form Interrogatories) and has provided the information of all witnesses that she presently has knowledge of." Based on these representations, the burden of responding to the Special Interrogatories outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of new /additional evidence. CCP 2017.020. To permit Defendant to confirm these representations in a verification by Plaintiff, and to explore any specific topics for which Defendant may have need, the Court will permit Defendant to propound 15 additional special interrogatories (as offered by Plaintiff). Plaintiff's request for monetary sanctions is DENIED. The Court does not necessarily find that Special Interrogatories 36-98, 100-103, 110-113, and 119-127 are improper, but that responding to them would be unduly burdensome based on the specific representation that all information already has been provided. Therefore, Defendant acted with substantial justification in pursuing this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LAUREN D. LUCERO VS. LIANA TAHIR ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Protective Order And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF LAUREN LUCERO's Motion For Protective Order And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion for protective order is GRANTED in part. Attorney Alioto's letter of April 18, 2019, states that "Plaintiff has provided all documentary evidence in her possession, all facts currently known to her that support her contentions and damages (in response to Special Interrogatories as well as Form Interrogatories) and has provided the information of all witnesses that she presently has knowledge of." Based on these representations, the burden of responding to the Special Interrogatories outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of new/additional evidence. CCP 2017.020. To permit Defendant to confirm these representations in a verification by Plaintiff, and to explore any specific topics for which Defendant may have need, the Court will permit Defendant to propound 15 additional special interrogatories (as offered by Plaintiff). The request for judicial notice is DENIED. There is no apparent privity between parties in this matter and the parties in the other matter, so the request is essentially for the court to review an unpublished (and unpublishable) Superior Court order. The request for sanctions is DENIED. The Court does not necessarily find that Special Interrogatories 36-98, 100-103, 110-113, and 119-127 are improper, but that responding to them would be unduly burdensome based on the specific representation that all information already has been provided. Therefore, Defendant acted with substantial justification in opposing this motion. rulings.Case Title: ALFRED MORALES VS. WALGREEN CO AN ILLINOIS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Walgreen Co.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay The Proceedings; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT WALGREEN CO.'s Motion To Stay The Proceedings. Defendant Walgreen Co.'s motion to stay the action filed by Plaintiff Alfred Morales pending a ruling on class certification in the action Caves et al. v. Walgreen Co., Case No. 2:18-cv-02910-MCE-DB, in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of California is granted. The factors specified in Caiafa Prof. Law Corp. v. State Farm Fire & Cas. Co. (1993) 15 Cal.App.4th 800, guiding the court's discretion in considering a stay of the state action when there is a pending federal action covering the same subject matter, weigh heavily in favor of staying the instant action. Morales filed (but did not serve) this action in state court on October 17, 2018. The federal action was filed approximately two weeks later, on November 2, 2018. There are currently two putative class actions pending against the same defendant, asserting substantially similar claims on behalf of nearly identical classes of store managers, and seeking substantially the same relief. In the federal action, significant discovery has been conducted and a briefing schedule set for a hearing on a class certification motion, while the state action has not so advanced. The progress made in the federal court action, filed very close in time after the state action, favors a stay because of the "stage to which the proceedings in the other court have already advanced." (Caiafa Prof. Law Corp., 15 Cal.App.4th at 804.) Further, a stay will avoid the risk of unseemly conflict between the state court and the federal court in ruling on identical issues of law and fact, and will also serve the interests of judicial economy. (Id. at 807.) The fact that the federal action is pending in California rather than in some other state also favors a stay. (Id. at 804.) Plaintiff's choice of forum does not outweigh the interests in avoiding inconsistent rulings by the courts, potential confusion of putative class members, and duplicative efforts by the parties and the courts. "It is undoubtedly preferable from the point of view of the judicial system to resolve the instant dispute by one class action rather than by duplicate class actions in two jurisdictions. If two class actions were allowed to proceed simultaneously, the already overtaxed resources of two courts will have been devoted to the resolution of a single dispute. Judicial economy and efficiency would not be served by allowing both actions to proceed as class actions." (Schneider v. Vennard (1986) 183 Cal.App.3d 1340, 1348 [trial court properly denied class certification on basis of pending federal class action between the parties on the same claims].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Walgreen is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ALFRED MORALES VS. WALGREEN CO AN ILLINOIS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Walgreen Co.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Precluding Plaintiffs Counsel From Communicating With Putative Class Members Without Court Approval, Directing Productions Of Website Contents, And Directing Production Of List Of Putative Class Members That Contacted Plaintiffs Counsel Following Plaintiff Counsels Improper Correspondence; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT WALGREEN CO's Motion For An Order Precluding Plaintiffs Counsel From Communicating With Putative Class Members Without Court Approval, Directing Productions Of Website Contents, And Directing Production Of List Of Putative Class Members That Contacted Plaintiffs Counsel Following Plaintiff Counsels Improper Correspondence. Defendant Walgreen Co.'s motion for an order precluding plaintiff's counsel from communicating with putative class members without court approval, directing production of website contents, and directing production of list of putative class members that contacted plaintiff's counsel following the mailing of counsel's newletter appears to be mooted in part by the Court's ruling staying this action. To the degree it is not, it is denied as to all three requests. The Court's full tentative ruling is sent to the parties by email. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ANISH SUTHAR ET AL VS. ALL STAR HOSPITALITY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Amended Responses To Discovery And Production Of Documents And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 4, (part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling) PLAINTIFFs ANISH SUTHAR and RUMANA MINAPARA's Motion To Compel Amended Responses To Discovery And Production Of Documents And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: HEARING REQUIRED. Be prepared to address the following. Interrogatories 3, 7, 8: Further response required. If answers may be determined from reviewing records, it will be sufficient to refer to those records. CCP 2030.230. If Defendants do not have information sufficient to answer more specifically, they should so state. CCP 2030.220(c). Plaintiffs are entitled to verified responses by Defendants, including the representation in Attorney Tsai's letter of 4.12.2019 that Defendants did not require Plaintiff Suthar to record time. Interrogatories 4, 9, 10, 12: No further response required. Defendants assert Plaintiff Minapara did not work for them. This reasonably suggests they have no record of meal breaks, etc. Compelling further responses would elevate form over substance. RFP 5, 18, 19: Plaintiff Suthar is entitled to payroll records about him. Questions: Does Defendants' email system (or Counsel's review platform) permit a metadata export for email objects in Plaintiff Suthar's account? Does the check-in system permit a metadata export of actions taken by Plaintiff Suthar? Does Defendants' telephone system have the capability to report metadata of calls associated with Plaintiff Suthar? If the answer to any of these questions is yes, the Court is inclined to order such reports because they may show information about hours worked. If Plaintiff believes the contents of any specific emails/records identified from metadata are relevant, he may request them. General references to other "business records" and "documents signed by Plaintiff Suthar" are not reasonably particularized. Discovery must be for what is relevant and proportional to the wage-and-hour claims. The Court will not require Defendants to review all systems and records for anything that may refer to Plaintiff Suthar. As a former employee, Plaintiff Suthar has knowledge of records that are likely to support his wage-and-hour claims. If there are further systems or records that he believes would support his claims he may specify such systems or records and the parties should meet-and-confer. The Court is inclined to find that both parties have acted with substantial justification and deny sanctions. See Part 2 of 2 for entire tentative ruling. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: OMAR RADMAN AL-KHAMERI VS. JENNY SOLA-SONG LEE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Sever And Motion To Change Venue; Memorandum In Support; And Declaration Of Jose L. Reynoso
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANT JENNY LEE, SONG SOONYI's Motion To Sever And Motion To Change Venue. Defendants Jenny Lee and Song Soonyi's motions to change venue and to sever are denied. Venue is proper in this court because the action was filed in the county where one of the defendants resides. (Code Civ. Proc. § 395(a).) Moving defendants have not shown that Plaintiff improperly joined the resident defendant. "A defendant's residence for venue purposes may only be disregarded when the joinder is sham, against one having no real interest in the litigation, who is joined solely for the purpose of achieving venue in a particular county." (Buran Equipment Co. v. Superior Court (1987) 190 Cal.App.3d 1662, 1666.) Nor have they shown that the San Francisco resident co-defendant was not joined in good faith, as the complaint states a cause of action against him. (See Gutierrez v. Superior Court (1966) 243 Cal.App.2d 710, 725-26 ["the usual test by which to determine whether a defendant has been joined in good faith is whether or not the complaint states a cause of action against him."].) To the extent that Defendants' argument is based on misjoinder under Code of Civil Procedure § 379(c), it is ill-founded. "[A] plaintiff may join two independent and successive tortfeasors as defendants in a single action if he pleads facts showing that he entertains a reasonable doubt as to which defendant is liable for his injuries, or the extent to which each may be liable." (Landau v. Salam (1971) 4 Cal.3d 901, 903 [plaintiff, who claimed to have been injured as the result of both the negligent driving of an automobile and the negligent maintenance of property on a later date, properly joined defendants responsible for both incidents].) Plaintiff's complaint is not a "mixed action" because the same venue provision, section 395(a) of the Code of Civil Procedure, applies to plaintiff's motor vehicle and general negligence causes of action against all defendants. (Cf. Brown v. Superior Court (1984) 37 Cal.3d 477.) Defendants have failed to show that separate trials are warranted under Code of Civil Procedure § 1048. Any inconvenience of witnesses does not outweigh the risk of inconsistent rulings as to the extent each accident caused plaintiff's injuries and the duplication of effort in presenting evidence related to the medical treatment of these injuries and Plaintiff's alleged damages. Defendants' motion to transfer venue on the basis of inconvenience to witnesses and promotion of the ends of justice under Code of Civil Procedure section 397(c) is untimely because moving defendants have not yet filed an answer to the complaint. Such a motion cannot be made before an answer is filed. (Buran Equipment Co., 190 Cal.App.3d at 1665; Delgado v. Superior Court (1977) 74 Cal.App.3d 560, 562-563.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN P. MILLWARD VS. BLUE FLAME, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order And Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 12, PLAINTIFF JOHN MILLWARD's Hearing For Right To Attach Order And Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment. Plaintiff John P. Millward's application for a right to attach order and order for issuance of a writ of attachment against Defendant Treva Cooke is granted in the amount of $115,361.45, plus reasonable attorneys' fees in the amount of $11,333.33. Code of Civil Procedure section 484.010 provides for issuance of a right to attach order and writ of attachment upon filing of the complaint or any time thereafter. A court "shall issue a right to attach order" if it finds: (1) the claim upon which the attachment is based is one upon which an attachment may be issued; (2) the plaintiff has established the probable validity of the claim upon which the attachment is based; (3) the attachment is not sought for a purpose other than the recovery on the claim upon which the attachment is based; and (4) the amount to be secured by the attachment is greater than zero. (Code Civ. Proc. § 484.090(a).) Plaintiff has satisfied these criteria. Code of Civil Procedure section 483.010 allows for prejudgment attachment in actions involving claims for money damages not less than $500 that are based on contracts, so the first element is met. As explained below, Plaintiff has established probable validity of his claim under the Guaranty. Ms. Cooke has not identified any improper purpose for Plaintiff seeking attachment. And Plaintiff is seeking $115,361.45 plus attorneys' fees, an amount greater than zero. Ms. Cooke has not identified any reason why attachment is inappropriate. Her requests for additional discovery prior to attachment are inapposite as she has not identified a statutory requirement making discovery a prerequisite to attachment. Nor is there any need for Plaintiff to demonstrate urgency. Ms. Cooke's argument about her liability being conditioned on a finding of liability as to Volition also fails. Under the Guaranty, Ms. Cooke agreed to "unconditionally guarantee to [Plaintiff], irrespective of the genuineness, validity, regularity or enforceability of the Note or any other circumstances, the prompt payment of the initial $250,000 in principal paid under the Note, by acceleration or otherwise." (Millward Decl. 11, Exh. C.) "A guaranty is a separate and independent obligation from that of the principal debt." (United Central Bank v. Superior Court (2009) 179 Cal.App.4th 212, 215.) The unequivocal language of the Guaranty and the legal authority establishing that Ms. Cooke has an independent obligation to secure the initial $250,000.00 regardless of liability under the other contracts show the probable validity of Plaintiff's claim supporting his right to attachment. Therefore, Plaintiff is entitled to the requested attachment of $115,361.45, the outstanding amount of the $250,000.00 principal guaranteed by Ms. Cooke. Plaintiff is also entitled to attorneys' fees under Code of Civil Procedure section 482.110. Because the attachment order is only being granted as to Ms. Cooke and not against Defendants Blue Flame, LLC or Volition LLC, one-third of the requested attorneys' fees, in the amount of $11,333.33, is appropriate. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHOO KOON NG VS. DAMIEN QUINN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Choo Ng'S Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories And Demand For Inspection And Production Of Documents And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 29, 2019, Line 5, Plaintiff Choo Ng'S Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories And Demand For Inspection And Production Of Documents And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: HEARING REQUIRED. Be prepared to address the following: Defendant's statement that discovery is ongoing, does not close until September, and that Plaintiff may seek supplemental discovery does not address the Court orders requiring responses and production by specific dates. Why should the Court not conclude that Defendant failed to comply with those orders? RFP 1, 2, 5: Why is the response limited to "checks"? Have all responsive documents been produced? If not, what has Defendant done to acquire responsive documents? Defendant is reminded that he is responsible for obtaining records under his "control." CCP 2031.010. RFP 3, 4, 6: The Code requires "a diligent search and a reasonable inquiry" for documents within a party's possession, custody, or control, and a statement about the basis for an inability to comply. CCP 2031.230. The limitation to "at this time" raises a question about the sufficiency of the search and compliance with earlier orders. Defendant should address what search has been done to comply. Has Defendant paid the monetary sanctions previously ordered? The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
